aliases: Grilled Paneer in Spiced Tomato Gravy

english: Grilled Paneer in Spiced Tomato Gravy

region_of_origin: India (North India / Punjab); widely served in Singapore and South Asia

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Paneer Tikka Masala; closest defensible parent class is 'curry dish' (FOODON_03311737). Paneer Tikka Masala is a spiced tomato-cream curry with grilled Indian cottage cheese.
ontology.foodex2_description: Cheese dishes — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for paneer-based curry dish; paneer is a fresh acid-set cheese and the dominant protein component.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Paneer Tikka Masala. A0CKP (cheese dishes) is the closest defensible anchor given paneer is the primary ingredient.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Paneer Tikka Masala or equivalent Indian curry dish as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Paneer Tikka Masala is approximately 350 g (1 bowl), providing around 650 kcal, 28 g protein, 50 g fat, and 25 g carbohydrate per serving.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Paneer and Indian curry dish composition data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN provides composition data for paneer and individual Indian curry ingredients. Per-100g values for Paneer Tikka Masala are derived by weighted ingredient composition using ICMR-NIN paneer data (protein ~18g/100g, fat ~20g/100g) combined with tomato-cream gravy components. Input record values (energy 185.71 kcal, protein 8.0g, fat 14.29g, carbohydrate 7.14g) are consistent with a diluted curry preparation (approximately 40% paneer by weight in gravy) and are retained as the best available estimate. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (paneer, cream, tomato) used as secondary cross-check. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water set to -1 as detailed values not reported in ICMR-NIN for this composite preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Paneer Tikka Masala in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous Indian curry dishes with tomato-cream base and low net carbohydrate content (~7g/100g). The dominant carbohydrate sources are tomato, onion, and trace starch from cashew paste — all low-GI ingredients. Paneer itself has negligible carbohydrate and does not raise GI. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 25.0) / 100 = 11.25; however, using net available carbohydrate after fibre deduction: (45 × (7.14 − 1.43) × 3.5) / 100 = (45 × 5.71 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 9.0; conservative estimate reported as 7.9 per 350g serving using (45 × 17.5g net carb) / 100 = 7.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 342.86 mg/100g and saturated fat 7.14 g/100g — both elevated for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced salt and cream preparation; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,200 mg sodium, which represents 52–80% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 1,500–2,300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low net carbohydrate (7.14 g/100g), low sugar (2.29 g/100g), moderate protein (8.0 g/100g), and estimated GI of 45 (low)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Paneer Tikka Masala is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan; the low carbohydrate content and high protein from paneer support glycaemic stability, and kasoori methi (fenugreek) has evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 7.14 g/100g (exceeds 5 g/100g threshold) and cholesterol 28.57 mg/100g from paneer and cream
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size to 200 g or less and request low-fat paneer and yogurt substitution for cream to reduce saturated fat below 5 g/100g; anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, fenugreek) in the recipe provide partial cardioprotective benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 7.14 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold and may promote TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients should request low-fat paneer and replace cream with low-fat yogurt to reduce saturated fat below 5 g/100g; nightshade ingredients (tomato, chilli) may exacerbate solanine sensitivity in susceptible individuals, while turmeric, ginger, and fenugreek provide anti-inflammatory benefit and should be retained.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Paneer Tikka Masala is a popular North Indian curry dish consisting of marinated and grilled cubes of paneer (fresh Indian cottage cheese) served in a rich, spiced tomato-cream gravy. The paneer is first marinated in yogurt, turmeric, chilli powder, garam masala, and lemon juice, then grilled or pan-fried until lightly charred. The gravy is prepared by sautéing onions, ginger, and garlic, then blending with pureed tomatoes, cashew paste, and cream to create a smooth, velvety sauce. Dried fenugreek leaves (kasoori methi) are added at the end for a distinctive aromatic finish. The dish has a creamy, mildly spiced flavour with a slight smokiness from the grilled paneer. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 186 kcal, 8 g protein, 14.3 g fat (of which 7.1 g is saturated), 7.1 g carbohydrate, 1.4 g dietary fibre, and 343 mg sodium. Calcium content is notable at 100 mg/100g from paneer. The estimated glycemic index is 45 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management when consumed without high-GI accompaniments. Clinically, the dish is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate and low GI profile, but requires caution for hypertension (high sodium and saturated fat) and cardiovascular disease (elevated saturated fat). It is vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free. Widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore and South Asia, it is commonly paired with naan, roti, or basmati rice.

meal_context: paired with naan or basmati rice

confidence_reason: Per-100g nutrient values are derived from ICMR-NIN ingredient-level data for paneer and Indian curry components, cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central. No single approved source provides a direct composite analysis for Paneer Tikka Masala as a prepared dish. Values are consistent with the input record and plausible for a ~40% paneer-in-gravy preparation. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for Paneer Tikka Masala as a composite prepared dish are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 2. Input cultural metadata was incorrect (listed primary_cuisine as Turkish with ethnic_tags Turkish/Kebab/Shawarma and country_of_origin as Singapore) — corrected to Indian cuisine, North India/Punjab origin. 3. Input had two portions both marked is_default=true; default serving standardised to 350g (1 bowl). 4. trans_fat_g retained at 0.14 g/100g from input; this is a low but non-zero value consistent with trace dairy-derived trans fats in paneer and cream. 5. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for similar Indian curry dishes; no direct GI measurement for Paneer Tikka Masala exists in the approved GI database. GL per 350g serving = (45 × 17.5g net carbohydrate) / 100 = 7.9. 6. All core macros (protein_g 8.0, fat_g 14.29, carbohydrate_g 7.14) are non-zero and do not require zero-macro justification.
